title: It is a way of communicating with the docker architecture and docker components. It allows us to send commands to the docker server (docker deamon). It doesn't do anything related to dockers except of sending the messages further to be executed.
What is a docker client (docker CLI)?
It is a tool responsible for creating images, running containers etc. - a heart of the docker ecosystem.
What is a docker deamon (docker server)?
It is a repository of container images, including containers from other developers.
It goes with a few simple steps:
docker client sends the information to the docker server to get the image.
docker server checks whether there is a local image available in so called 'image cache'.
if so, it runs that image, if not, it tells us that there is no local copy of that image
it next reaches out to the docker hub, downloads that image, caches it locally and runs it.

(Unix) Operating systems use kernel to communicate between running processes and hardware. The process makes 'system call' that is processed by kernel.
Namespacing is segmenting a part of the hard disk to host a specific environment, e.g. with a specific python version. It enables two programs running in different environments to run together on the same system. Above is one of the uses of namespacing.
Control groups are used to limit amount of resources dedicated to a single process.
These two are used together to create a 'separated' part of the hardware that is used by selected processes.

It is a process or family of processes that have a separated group of resources specifically assigned to it. It is also a 'running image' - it is an instance of the image, concept similar to object of a class.

It is a snapshot of the file system used to run a certain program/programs with a running command that is executed to run the container. When running the container based on an image, the kernel is isolating a specific part of hard drive for the container and puts the resources there. Next, the running command is executed.
Docker is running on windows/mac via linux virtual machine host, that's why it's possible to use the idea of namespacing.
By using the
docker run <image-name> <command>
c
syntax. For instance
docker run busybox ls
c
will list the files in the snapshot of busybox docker image.
How can you override the docker run command?

Notice that the command is executed inside of the container environment. It means that only the commands that are included in the filesystem snapshot of a docker image will be executed; otherwise it will print an error message. For example, the hello-world docker image has only one program inside, hence 'echo' or 'ls' functions will fail to work when overriding the run command.
By using
docker ps
c
command. To see all of the containers ever created, execute
docker ps --all
c
How can you list docker containers currently running?
It runs
docker create
c
docker start
c
. Using
docker create
c
prints the id of the created docker (it only maps the snapshot to the hard drive). Next,
docker start <id>
c
runs the container.
docker start -a <id>
c
also prints the output from the run command and container logs(if there are any).
We can use
docker start
c
to run again the exited container. However, we cannot override the run command that was used when first creating the container. Hence, after using
docker run
c
or
docker create
c
we can only re-run that container without changing the command.
Use
docker run -d <id>
c
to run the docker in the background and be able to still use the terminal to run some commands. Same goes for
docker-compose up -d
c
and other commands that are blocking the terminal.
By using
docker system prune
c
command. Be wary, it also will remove them irreversibly with all cached images also. To remove single container, run
docker container rm <id>
c
How can we delete all of the created containers?
How can we get information about container?
The first one asks container 'nicely' to stop. It gives it 10 second to finish process before sending the sigkill. The other stops the container immediately.
It is done by using
docker exec -it <id> <command>
c
syntax. The
-i
c
flag allows the user to interact with the docker (opens stdin channel), while
-t
c
flag conveys the output in a 'terminal-like' manner.
How can we run a single command (another process) inside of running container?
docker exec -it <id> sh
c
opens up the shell terminal mode inside of the container (if it's available). This allows us to type multiple commands just like in unix shell.
docker run -it <image-name> sh
c

is overriding the basic run command with the shell init command. So it doesn't run the primary run command but allows us to issue some command in terminal right away.
Quit the shell with
ctrl+d
c
Specifying a base image that we will build on (command
FROM
c
Running additional commands on top of the base image (command
RUN
c
Adding a run command for container startup (command
CMD
c

The docker building runs command by command from Dockerfile and creates temporary docker containers, runs commands inside and creates snapshots. It also caches the in-between docker images so they can be used for another build.
To build a docker image run
docker build .
c
command.
To tag the docker (so we can use arbitrary name instead of the id) we can use building with
docker build -t maciejdomagala/<image-name>:latest .
c
command to tag our docker with the latest version and call it by the name.
To define a Dockerfile that will be used for a guild we should use the
-f
c
flag. E.g
docker build -f Dockerfile.dev
c
Describe docker building using a Dockerfile.
We can create docker images from running containers. We are doing that by using the command
docker commit -c 'CMD ["<run-command>"]' <existing-container-id>
c

This creates an image with existing container as base and arbitrary run-command specified above. Even tho it is possible to use that, it is advised to use Dockerfile type of image creating for consistency purposes.
Describe building using existing image (not Dockerfile)
By using
COPY <src> <dest>
c
syntax inside Dockerfile. If we are running Dockerfile in the project folder
COPY ./ ./
c
would do.
How do we copy local files to the container?
By pointing to specific directory when using
COPY
c
or by using
WORKDIR <path>
c
command. The latter is setting up the directory from which the commands inside the container will be run. Hence
COPY ./ ./
c
would actually copy the files from
c
locally to
<path>/
c
How can we choose the place where files are headed when copying?

Docker compose is a docker tool used in multi-container applications. It is making cumbersome and repetitive using of docker CLI automatic. It is used in a form of YAML file (named docker-compose.yaml). The file contains configuration of services necessary for the whole app that is based on several containers.
We use docker compose as a more suitable way of executing the docker cli commands by moving them to the .yaml file.
Docker manages single container, docker-compose - multi-containers.
What is Docker Compose and why do we use it?
We state the version of compose tool. More on that here:
https://docs.docker.com/compose/compose-file/compose-versioning/
a
https://docs.docker.com/compose/compose-file/compose-versioning/

Services would be the containers to run in parallel that we need for the app. One of them is redis-server run from the 'redis' image, second is the node-app, which is built from the working directory Dockerfile.
For the node-app container we also state the ports that will be mapped from local port to the container port.
Use
docker-compose up
c
and
docker-compose down
c
to start and stop the container network defined in docker-compose.yaml. Use the
-d
c
flag to run it in the background.
default 'no' - never try to restart the network.
'on failure' - only restart when we get status code other than 0.
'unless-stopped' - try to restart unless the developer stopped it.
What are the restart policies for docker-compose?
Use
docker-compose ps
c
from the directory with docker-compose.yaml file to get information about the containers status (containers linked to docker-compose.yaml file).

98uZ: Notes made while getting more familiar with docker functionalities.
title: Docker basics Q&A
